'''念仏寺'''（ねんぶつじ）は[[奈良県]][[奈良市]]漢国町7にある[[浄土宗]]の[[寺院]]である。[[電話番号]]は0742-22-5627。[[山号]]は降魔山
== 歴史 == [[元和]]元年（[[1615年]]）に[[袋中]]が当時[[伏見城]]代[[松平定勝]]（隠岐守）を開基として創建
== 境内 == *阿弥陀如来像（木造で[[鎌倉時代]]に造られた） *地蔵菩薩像（石造で[[室町時代]]に造られた） *金春家の[[墓]]
== アクセス == *JR・近鉄[[奈良駅]]下車。
{{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:奈良県の寺]] [[Category:奈良市]]

'''念仏寺'''（ねんぶつじ）は[[奈良県]][[奈良市]]にある[[浄土宗]]の[[寺院]]である。[[山号]]は降魔山。院号は善光院。
== 歴史 == [[元和 (日本)|元和]]８年（[[1622年]]）に[[袋中]]が草創した。当時の[[伏見城]]代[[松平定勝]]（隠岐守）が檀越。 [[寛永]]年間に袋中が一切経を蒐集・書写し、それらを念仏寺の経蔵に納めた。一切経は江戸末期に散逸。同じ頃無住となり、開化天皇陵の造営のため境内地の一部を接収され、寺運衰退するが、後に入った住持により復興する。
== 境内 == *阿弥陀如来像（木造で[[鎌倉時代]]に造られた） *地蔵菩薩像（石造で[[室町時代]]に造られた） *金春家の[[墓]]
== 所在地 == *奈良県奈良市漢国町7 *電話番号：0742-22-5627
== アクセス == *[[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[奈良駅]]下車。
{{DEFAULTSORT:ねんふつし}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:浄土宗の寺院]] [[Category:奈良市の寺]]

'''念仏寺'''（ねんぶつじ）は[[奈良県]][[奈良市]]にある[[浄土宗]]の[[寺院]]である。[[山号]]は降魔山。院号は善光院。
== 歴史 == [[元和 (日本)|元和]]8年（[[1622年]]）に[[袋中]]が草創した。当時の[[伏見城]]代[[松平定勝]]（隠岐守）が檀越。 [[寛永]]年間に袋中が一切経を蒐集・書写し、それらを念仏寺の経蔵に納めた。一切経は江戸末期に散逸。同じ頃無住となり、開化天皇陵の造営のため境内地の一部を接収され、寺運衰退するが、後に入った住持により復興する。
== 境内 == *阿弥陀如来像（木造で[[鎌倉時代]]に造られた） *地蔵菩薩像（石造で[[室町時代]]に造られた） *金春家の[[墓]]
== 所在地 == *奈良県奈良市漢国町7 *電話番号：0742-22-5627
== アクセス == *[[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[奈良駅]]下車。

'''念仏寺'''（ねんぶつじ）は、[[奈良県]][[奈良市]]にある[[浄土宗]]の[[寺院]]である。[[山号]]は降魔山。院号は善光院。
== 歴史 == [[元和 (日本)|元和]]8年（[[1622年]]）に[[袋中]]が草創した。当時の[[伏見城]]代[[松平定勝]]（隠岐守）が檀越。 [[寛永]]年間に袋中が一切経を蒐集・書写し、それらを念仏寺の経蔵に納めた。一切経は江戸末期に散逸。同じ頃無住となり、開化天皇陵の造営のため境内地の一部を接収され、寺運衰退するが、後に入った住持により復興する。
== 境内 == *阿弥陀如来像（木造で[[鎌倉時代]]に造られた） *地蔵菩薩像（石造で[[室町時代]]に造られた） *金春家の[[墓]]
== 所在地 == *奈良県奈良市漢国町7
== アクセス == *[[西日本旅客鉄道|JR]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[奈良駅]]下車。
{{デフォルトソート:ねんふつし}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:浄土宗の寺院]] [[Category:奈良市の寺]]

revision.text:
{{Citation required|date=October 2012|sort key=Temple}} {{Japanese Temple |Name = {{ruby|Nenbutsu-ji|念佛寺}} |image = [[File:Nembutsu-ji (Nara).JPG|240px]] |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}{{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町}} 7 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|1|N|135|49|28.7|E|region:JP}} |Mountain Name = Gōmazan |Temple Title = Zenkō-in |Sect = {{ill|Jōdo-shū|ja|浄土宗}} |Head Deity = {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}} |Foundation = Genna 8 (1622) |Founder = {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中}} |Formal Name = Gōmazan Zenkō-in Nenbutsu-ji<ref>[http://otera.jodo.or.jp/temple/30-013/ Jōdo-shū Temple Introduction Navi]</ref> |Alternate Names = Mountain Temple }}
'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a {{ill|Jōdo-shū|ja|浄土宗}} temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.
== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of {{ill|Matsudaira Tadayoshi|ja|松平定勝}}, a half-brother of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}}. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by {{ill|Sanada Yukimura|ja|真田信繁}} during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the {{ill|Taima Mandala|ja|当麻曼荼羅}}. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.
== Features ==
* Wooden standing statue of {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来}} (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}} (Muromachi period) * Memorial to {{ill|Matsudaira Tadayoshi|ja|松平定勝}} * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅}} ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68416,135.82587|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}}) * 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the {{ill|Nara Kōtsū|ja|奈良交通}} City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68449,135.82717|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}}) * 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅}} ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68113,135.81943|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}})
== References == {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}}

* Wooden standing statue of {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}} (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}} (Muromachi period) * Memorial to {{ill|Matsudaira Tadayoshi|ja|松平定勝|12=draft}} * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=draft}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅|12=draft}} ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68416,135.82587|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}}) * 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the {{ill|Nara Kōtsū|ja|奈良交通|12=draft}} City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68449,135.82717|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}}) * 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅|12=draft}} ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68113,135.81943|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}})

revision.text:
{{Citation required|date=October 2012|sort key=Temple}} {{Japanese Temple |Name = {{ruby|Nenbutsu-ji|念佛寺}} |image = [[File:Nembutsu-ji (Nara).JPG|240px]] |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}{{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市|12=draft}}{{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=draft}} 7 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|1|N|135|49|28.7|E|region:JP}} |Mountain Name = Gōmazan |Temple Title = Zenkō-in |Sect = {{ill|Jōdo-shū|ja|浄土宗|12=draft}} |Head Deity = {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}} |Foundation = Genna 8 (1622) |Founder = {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=draft}} |Formal Name = Gōmazan Zenkō-in Nenbutsu-ji<ref>[http://otera.jodo.or.jp/temple/30-013/ Jōdo-shū Temple Introduction Navi]</ref> |Alternate Names = Mountain Temple }}
'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a {{ill|Jōdo-shū|ja|浄土宗|12=draft}} temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.
== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of {{ill|Matsudaira Tadayoshi|ja|松平定勝|12=draft}}, a half-brother of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft}}. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by {{ill|Sanada Yukimura|ja|真田信繁|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=draft}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=draft}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=draft}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the {{ill|Taima Mandala|ja|当麻曼荼羅|12=draft}}. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=draft}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.
== Features ==
* Wooden standing statue of {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}} (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}} (Muromachi period) * Memorial to {{ill|Matsudaira Tadayoshi|ja|松平定勝|12=draft}} * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=draft}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅|12=draft}} ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68416,135.82587|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}}) * 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the {{ill|Nara Kōtsū|ja|奈良交通|12=draft}} City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68449,135.82717|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}}) * 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅|12=draft}} ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68113,135.81943|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}})

== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅|12=draft}} ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68416,135.82587|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}}) * 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the {{ill|Nara Kōtsū|ja|奈良交通|12=draft}} City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68449,135.82717|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}}) * 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅|12=draft}} ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68113,135.81943|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}})

revision.text:
{{Citation required|date=October 2012|sort key=Temple}} {{Japanese Temple |Name = {{ruby|Nenbutsu-ji|念佛寺}} |image = [[File:Nembutsu-ji (Nara).JPG|240px]] |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}{{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市|12=draft}}{{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=draft}} 7 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|1|N|135|49|28.7|E|region:JP}} |Mountain Name = Gōmazan |Temple Title = Zenkō-in |Sect = {{ill|Jōdo-shū|ja|浄土宗|12=draft}} |Head Deity = {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}} |Foundation = Genna 8 (1622) |Founder = {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=draft}} |Formal Name = Gōmazan Zenkō-in Nenbutsu-ji<ref>[http://otera.jodo.or.jp/temple/30-013/ Jōdo-shū Temple Introduction Navi]</ref> |Alternate Names = Mountain Temple }}
'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a {{ill|Jōdo-shū|ja|浄土宗|12=draft}} temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.
== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of {{ill|Matsudaira Tadayoshi|ja|松平定勝|12=draft}}, a half-brother of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft}}. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=draft}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by {{ill|Sanada Yukimura|ja|真田信繁|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=draft}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=draft}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=draft}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the {{ill|Taima Mandala|ja|当麻曼荼羅|12=draft}}. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=draft}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.
== Features ==
* Wooden standing statue of {{ill|Amida Nyorai|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}} (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}} (Muromachi period) * Memorial to {{ill|Matsudaira Tadayoshi|ja|松平定勝|12=draft}} * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=draft}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|ja|近鉄奈良駅|12=draft}} ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68416,135.82587|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}}) * 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the {{ill|Nara Kōtsū|ja|奈良交通|12=draft}} City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68449,135.82717|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}}) * 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of {{ill|JR Nara Station|ja|奈良駅|12=draft}} ({{OSM Directions|start=34.68113,135.81943|goal=34.68360,135.82464|name=Route Guide}})

'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.
== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], a half-brother of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|qq=|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by [[:en:Sanada Yukimura|Sanada Yukimura]] during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|qq=|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|qq=|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the [[:en:Taima mandala|Taima Mandala]]. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|qq=|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.
== Features ==
* Wooden standing statue of [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]] (Muromachi period) * Memorial to [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]] * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, [[:en:Kintetsu Nara Station|Kintetsu Nara Station]]
* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the [[:en:Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|Nara Kōtsū]] City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line
* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of [[:en:Nara Station|JR Nara Station]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nenbutsu-ji (Kangokuchō, Nara)]][[ja:念仏寺 (奈良市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:23, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation required|date=October 2012|sort key=Temple}} {{Japanese Temple |Name = {{ruby|Nenbutsu-ji|念佛寺}} |image = [[File:Nembutsu-ji (Nara).JPG|240px]] |Location = [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]][[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]]{{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 7 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|1|N|135|49|28.7|E|region:JP}} |Mountain Name = Gōmazan |Temple Title = Zenkō-in |Sect = [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] |Head Deity = [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] |Foundation = Genna 8 (1622) |Founder = {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}} |Formal Name = Gōmazan Zenkō-in Nenbutsu-ji<ref>[http://otera.jodo.or.jp/temple/30-013/ Jōdo-shū Temple Introduction Navi]</ref> |Alternate Names = Mountain Temple }}
'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.
== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], a half-brother of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by [[:en:Sanada Yukimura|Sanada Yukimura]] during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the [[:en:Taima mandala|Taima Mandala]]. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.
== Features ==
* Wooden standing statue of [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]] (Muromachi period) * Memorial to [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]] * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, [[:en:Kintetsu Nara Station|Kintetsu Nara Station]]
* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the [[:en:Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|Nara Kōtsū]] City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line
* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of [[:en:Nara Station|JR Nara Station]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Jōdo-shū temples]]
[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the [[:en:Taima mandala|Taima Mandala]]. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.

'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.
== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], a half-brother of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by [[:en:Sanada Yukimura|Sanada Yukimura]] during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the [[:en:Taima mandala|Taima Mandala]]. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.

* Wooden standing statue of [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]] (Muromachi period) * Memorial to [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]] * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nenbutsu-ji (Kangokuchō, Nara)]][[ja:念仏寺 (奈良市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:23, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation required|date=October 2012|sort key=Temple}} {{Japanese Temple |Name = {{ruby|Nenbutsu-ji|念佛寺}} |image = [[File:Nembutsu-ji (Nara).JPG|240px]] |Location = [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]][[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]]{{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 7 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|1|N|135|49|28.7|E|region:JP}} |Mountain Name = Gōmazan |Temple Title = Zenkō-in |Sect = [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] |Head Deity = [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] |Foundation = Genna 8 (1622) |Founder = {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}} |Formal Name = Gōmazan Zenkō-in Nenbutsu-ji<ref>[http://otera.jodo.or.jp/temple/30-013/ Jōdo-shū Temple Introduction Navi]</ref> |Alternate Names = Mountain Temple }}
'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.
== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], a half-brother of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by [[:en:Sanada Yukimura|Sanada Yukimura]] during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the [[:en:Taima mandala|Taima Mandala]]. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.
== Features ==
* Wooden standing statue of [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]] (Muromachi period) * Memorial to [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]] * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, [[:en:Kintetsu Nara Station|Kintetsu Nara Station]]
* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the [[:en:Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|Nara Kōtsū]] City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line
* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of [[:en:Nara Station|JR Nara Station]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Jōdo-shū temples]]
[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], a half-brother of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by [[:en:Sanada Yukimura|Sanada Yukimura]] during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the [[:en:Taima mandala|Taima Mandala]]. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.
== Features ==
* Wooden standing statue of [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]] (Muromachi period) * Memorial to [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]] * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, [[:en:Kintetsu Nara Station|Kintetsu Nara Station]]
* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the [[:en:Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|Nara Kōtsū]] City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line
* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of [[:en:Nara Station|JR Nara Station]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}}

[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.
== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], a half-brother of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by [[:en:Sanada Yukimura|Sanada Yukimura]] during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the [[:en:Taima mandala|Taima Mandala]]. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.
== Features ==
* Wooden standing statue of [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]] (Muromachi period) * Memorial to [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]] * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, [[:en:Kintetsu Nara Station|Kintetsu Nara Station]]
* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the [[:en:Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|Nara Kōtsū]] City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line
* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of [[:en:Nara Station|JR Nara Station]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}}

* Wooden standing statue of [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]] (Muromachi period) * Memorial to [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]] * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, [[:en:Kintetsu Nara Station|Kintetsu Nara Station]]
* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the [[:en:Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|Nara Kōtsū]] City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line
* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of [[:en:Nara Station|JR Nara Station]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nenbutsu-ji (Kangokuchō, Nara)]][[ja:念仏寺 (奈良市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nenbutsu-ji (Kangokuchō, Nara)]][[ja:念仏寺 (奈良市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nenbutsu-ji (Kangokuchō, Nara)]][[ja:念仏寺 (奈良市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:23, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation required|date=October 2012|sort key=Temple}} {{Japanese Temple |Name = {{ruby|Nenbutsu-ji|念佛寺}} |image = [[File:Nembutsu-ji (Nara).JPG|240px]] |Location = [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]][[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]]{{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 7 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|1|N|135|49|28.7|E|region:JP}} |Mountain Name = Gōmazan |Temple Title = Zenkō-in |Sect = [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] |Head Deity = [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] |Foundation = Genna 8 (1622) |Founder = {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}} |Formal Name = Gōmazan Zenkō-in Nenbutsu-ji<ref>[http://otera.jodo.or.jp/temple/30-013/ Jōdo-shū Temple Introduction Navi]</ref> |Alternate Names = Mountain Temple }}
'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.
== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], a half-brother of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by [[:en:Sanada Yukimura|Sanada Yukimura]] during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the [[:en:Taima mandala|Taima Mandala]]. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.
== Features ==
* Wooden standing statue of [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]] (Muromachi period) * Memorial to [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]] * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, [[:en:Kintetsu Nara Station|Kintetsu Nara Station]]
* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the [[:en:Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|Nara Kōtsū]] City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line
* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of [[:en:Nara Station|JR Nara Station]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nenbutsu-ji (Kangokuchō, Nara)]][[ja:念仏寺 (奈良市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nenbutsu-ji (Kangokuchō, Nara)]][[ja:念仏寺 (奈良市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nenbutsu-ji (Kangokuchō, Nara)]][[ja:念仏寺 (奈良市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:23, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation required|date=October 2012|sort key=Temple}} {{Japanese Temple |Name = {{ruby|Nenbutsu-ji|念佛寺}} |image = [[File:Nembutsu-ji (Nara).JPG|240px]] |Location = [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]][[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]]{{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 7 |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|41|1|N|135|49|28.7|E|region:JP}} |Mountain Name = Gōmazan |Temple Title = Zenkō-in |Sect = [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] |Head Deity = [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] |Foundation = Genna 8 (1622) |Founder = {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}} |Formal Name = Gōmazan Zenkō-in Nenbutsu-ji<ref>[http://otera.jodo.or.jp/temple/30-013/ Jōdo-shū Temple Introduction Navi]</ref> |Alternate Names = Mountain Temple }}
'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a [[:en:Jōdo-shū|Jōdo-shū]] temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.
== History ==
* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]], a half-brother of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by [[:en:Sanada Yukimura|Sanada Yukimura]] during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the [[:en:Taima mandala|Taima Mandala]]. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref> * During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref> * In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.
== Features ==
* Wooden standing statue of [[:en:Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (Kamakura period) * Stone statue of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]] (Muromachi period) * Memorial to [[:en:Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|Matsudaira Tadayoshi]] * Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara * Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion * Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}
== Access == * 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, [[:en:Kintetsu Nara Station|Kintetsu Nara Station]]
* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the [[:en:Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|Nara Kōtsū]] City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line
* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of [[:en:Nara Station|JR Nara Station]] == References == {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{Commonscat}}

The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref> * According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by [[:en:Sanada Yukimura|Sanada Yukimura]] during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara. * Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the [[:en:Taima mandala|Taima Mandala]]. '''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|WD=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū}} temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.

== History ==

* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of {{ill|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|en|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|WD=Q3136471|lt=Matsudaira Tadayoshi}}, a half-brother of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref>
* According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by {{ill|Sanada Yukimura|en|Sanada Yukimura|WD=Q3948081|lt=Sanada Yukimura}} during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara.
* Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the {{ill|Taima mandala|en|Taima mandala|WD=Q3514039|lt=Taima Mandala}}. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref>
* During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref>
* In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.

== Features ==

* Wooden standing statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} (Kamakura period)
* Stone statue of {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu}} (Muromachi period)
* Memorial to {{ill|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|en|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|WD=Q3136471|lt=Matsudaira Tadayoshi}}
* Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara
* Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion
* Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}

== Access ==
* 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|en|Kintetsu Nara Station|WD=Q6414371|lt=Kintetsu Nara Station}} 

* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the {{ill|Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|en|Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|WD=Q11445000|lt=Nara Kōtsū}} City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line 

* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of {{ill|Nara Station|en|Nara Station|WD=Q1150978|lt=JR Nara Station}}

|Founder = {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}
|Formal Name = Gōmazan Zenkō-in Nenbutsu-ji<ref>[http://otera.jodo.or.jp/temple/30-013/ Jōdo-shū Temple Introduction Navi]</ref>
|Alternate Names = Mountain Temple
}}

'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|WD=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū}} temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.

== History ==

* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of {{ill|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|en|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|WD=Q3136471|lt=Matsudaira Tadayoshi}}, a half-brother of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref>
* According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by {{ill|Sanada Yukimura|en|Sanada Yukimura|WD=Q3948081|lt=Sanada Yukimura}} during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara.
* Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the {{ill|Taima mandala|en|Taima mandala|WD=Q3514039|lt=Taima Mandala}}. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref>
* During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref>
* In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.

== Features ==

* Wooden standing statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} (Kamakura period)
* Stone statue of {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu}} (Muromachi period)
* Memorial to {{ill|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|en|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|WD=Q3136471|lt=Matsudaira Tadayoshi}}
* Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara
* Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion
* Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}

== Access ==
* 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|en|Kintetsu Nara Station|WD=Q6414371|lt=Kintetsu Nara Station}} 

* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the {{ill|Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|en|Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|WD=Q11445000|lt=Nara Kōtsū}} City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line 

* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of {{ill|Nara Station|en|Nara Station|WD=Q1150978|lt=JR Nara Station}}

{{Reflist|2}}
== See Also ==

'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|WD=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū}} temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.

== History ==

* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of {{ill|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|en|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|WD=Q3136471|lt=Matsudaira Tadayoshi}}, a half-brother of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref>
* According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by {{ill|Sanada Yukimura|en|Sanada Yukimura|WD=Q3948081|lt=Sanada Yukimura}} during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara.
* Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the {{ill|Taima mandala|en|Taima mandala|WD=Q3514039|lt=Taima Mandala}}. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref>
* During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref>
* In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.

== Features ==

* Wooden standing statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai}} (Kamakura period)
* Stone statue of {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu}} (Muromachi period)
* Memorial to {{ill|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|en|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|WD=Q3136471|lt=Matsudaira Tadayoshi}}
* Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara
* Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion
* Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}

== Access ==
* 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|en|Kintetsu Nara Station|WD=Q6414371|lt=Kintetsu Nara Station}} 

* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the {{ill|Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|en|Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|WD=Q11445000|lt=Nara Kōtsū}} City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line 

* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of {{ill|Nara Station|en|Nara Station|WD=Q1150978|lt=JR Nara Station}} 
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== See Also ==
{{Commonscat}}

'''Nenbutsu-ji''' (念佛寺) is a {{ill|Jōdo-shū|en|Jōdo-shū|WD=Q912208|lt=Jōdo-shū|1=Jōdo-shū}} temple located in {{ill|Kangoku-chō|ja|漢国町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangoku-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. Its mountain name (''sangō'') is '''Gōmazan''' and its temple title (''ingō'') is '''Zenkō-in'''.

== History ==

* Nenbutsu-ji was founded in Genna 8 (1622) at the order of {{ill|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|en|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|WD=Q3136471|lt=Matsudaira Tadayoshi|1=Hisamatsu Sadakatsu}}, a half-brother of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}. The temple’s founder was the monk {{ill|Fukushū|ja|袋中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukushū|1=Taichū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11626521}}.<ref>[https://narakanko-enjoy.com/?p=11497 "Nenbutsu-ji: A Temple Linked to Ieyasu, Just Steps from Kintetsu Nara Station"]</ref>
* According to folklore, Tokugawa Ieyasu, pursued by {{ill|Sanada Yukimura|en|Sanada Yukimura|WD=Q3948081|lt=Sanada Yukimura|1=Sanada Yukimura}} during the {{ill|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja|大坂冬の陣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大坂の陣|1=Siege of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q475780}}, hid inside a coffin at a cooper’s shop on the temple grounds to escape. While this tale is not confirmed, skirmishes between Ieyasu and Sanada’s forces near Kizu are recorded in the {{ill|Tokugawa Jikki|ja|徳川実紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Jikki|1=Tokugawa jikki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2439031}}. A similar story is associated with {{ill|Saijō-ji (Nara)|ja|西照寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijō-ji (Nara)|1=Saishō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q38277829}} in Nara.
* Fukushū’s disciple, Zenbiki, found a unique [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] mandala in a shop in Nara while searching for the {{ill|Taima mandala|en|Taima mandala|WD=Q3514039|lt=Taima Mandala|1=Taima mandala}}. This mandala, known as the ''Chikō Mandala,'' became a focal point for Fukushū, who settled at what was then called ''Kōbōzan'' to establish the temple. Early records refer to the site as ''Mimokuzan,'' and the temple’s original image of worship was a painted depiction of the {{ill|Zenkōji Amida Triad|ja|善光寺式阿弥陀三尊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenkōji Amida Triad|1=Amitabha triad in Zenkoji-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q986386}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Yasuhiko Inagaki|date=March 2020|title=Annotated and Republished: "History of Gōmazan Zenkō-ji" by Ryōjō (Fukushū)|journal=Bunkyo University Kokubun|volume=49|pages=11–48|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1351/00007618/}}</ref>
* During the Kan’ei era (1624–1644), Fukushū collected and transcribed a complete set of Buddhist scriptures, which were stored in the temple’s sutra repository. These scriptures were scattered by the late Edo period.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tetsusei Miyake|date=2008|title=The History and Current Status of Fukushū’s Complete Buddhist Scripture Collection|journal=Kokusai Bukkyōgaku Daigakuin Daigaku Kiyō|volume=12|pages=27–56|url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1153/00000045/}}</ref>
* In 1862 (Bunkyū 2), the sutra repository and cemetery were relocated during restoration efforts for the nearby {{ill|Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja|開化天皇陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kaika Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 開化天皇|1=Emperor Kaika|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333548}}. The temple declined during the late Edo and early Meiji periods but was later revived under new leadership.

== Features ==

* Wooden standing statue of {{ill|Amitābha|en|Amitābha|WD=Q236242|lt=Amida Nyorai|1=Amitābha}} (Kamakura period)
* Stone statue of {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|1=Kṣitigarbha}} (Muromachi period)
* Memorial to {{ill|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|en|Hisamatsu Sadakatsu|WD=Q3136471|lt=Matsudaira Tadayoshi|1=Hisamatsu Sadakatsu}}
* Grave of Kyūzan Motoyasu, magistrate of Nara
* Rokujimyōgō stele by Jion
* Graves of the {{ill|Konparu Family|ja|金春家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konparu Family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金春流|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}

== Access ==
* 2 minutes on foot from Exit 7-S, {{ill|Kintetsu Nara Station|en|Kintetsu Nara Station|WD=Q6414371|lt=Kintetsu Nara Station|1=Kintetsu Nara Station}} 

* 4 minutes on foot from "Kintetsu Nara Station" stop on the {{ill|Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|en|Nara Kotsu Bus Lines|WD=Q11445000|lt=Nara Kōtsū|1=Nara Kotsu Bus Lines}} City Loop (Outer Circle) bus line 

* 10 minutes on foot from the east exit of {{ill|Nara Station|en|Nara Station|WD=Q1150978|lt=JR Nara Station|1=Nara Station}} 
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== See Also ==
{{Commonscat}}
